# Further Linear Regression

\vspace{-1cm}

A straight-line relationship can be valuable in many situations in establishing and summarising the dependence of one variable on another, and in making predictions. Linear regression will be explored in more detail in this chapter.

### The Method of Least Squares Regression {-}

In most experiments, it is assumed that $x$ is under the control of the experimenter and can be known exactly. In such cases $x$ is called the \textit{predictor} (or \textit{independent}) variable, whilst $Y$ is a \textbf{random variable} known as the \textit{response} (or \textit{dependent}) variable.

\begin{multicols}{2}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (7,0) node[below] {$x$};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (0,5) node[left] {$y$};
        \node[below] at (3.5,-0.3) {independent variable};
        \node[rotate=90,above] at (-0.3,2.5) {response variable};
        \draw[very thick] (0.5,1.6) -- (5.5,4.2) node[right] {$y=\alpha+\beta x$};
        \draw[fill,densely dashed] (3.5258224,3.072553) circle (0.05) -- (3.5258224,3.173427648);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (3.3020558,2.331238) circle (0.05) -- (3.3020558,3.057069016);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (3.7090608,3.683062) circle (0.05) -- (3.7090608,3.268711616);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (4.3429324,4.169212) circle (0.05) -- (4.3429324,3.598324848);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (0.9282517,1.662861) circle (0.05) -- (0.9282517,1.822690884);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (3.9876255,3.250634) circle (0.05) -- (3.9876255,3.41356526);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (4.6912468,3.534086) circle (0.05) -- (4.6912468,3.779448336);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (1.8099240,2.297074) circle (0.05) -- (1.8099240,2.2811605);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (1.4651929,1.490792) circle (0.05) -- (1.4651929,2.101900308);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (2.2436259,2.614456) circle (0.05) -- (2.2436259,2.506685468);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (4.4783236,3.647896) circle (0.05) -- (4.4783236,3.668728272);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (3.9126398,2.929844) circle (0.05) -- (3.9126398,3.374572696);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (3.4281654,3.268362) circle (0.05) -- (3.4281654,3.122646008);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (1.3975460,2.601538) circle (0.05) -- (1.3975460,2.0667239);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (2.8836849,3.199075) circle (0.05) -- (2.8836849,2.839516148);
\draw[fill,densely dashed] (3.2025879,3.355931) circle (0.05) -- (3.2025879,3.005345708);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

The theoretical model for linear regression is that of a straight line of form $y=\alpha+\beta x$ describing the best fit for a relationship between variables. However, the population of bivariate data will not perfectly lie along this line due to chance variation.

\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=The Linear Regression Model,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=6.5cm]
\[Y_i=\alpha+\beta x_i+\varepsilon_i\]
\end{tcolorbox}
 
$\varepsilon$ (\textit{"epsilon"}) is a random variable called the \textbf{error} that describes how far $Y$ is from its expected value. 

\end{multicols}

The values of parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ cannot be known exactly without examining all possible occurrences of $Y$ and $x$. However the sample observations $(x_i,y_i)$ can be used to give estimates, $a$ and $b$, of $\alpha$ and $\beta$. This leads to the relationship $\hat{Y}_i=a+bx_i$, where $\hat{Y}_i$ (*"y-hat"*) denotes the predicted value of $Y_i$ for a given $x_i$.

The method of least squares gives formulae for $a$ and $b$ derived by considering how $\sum \varepsilon_i^2$ might be minimised:

\begin{multicols}{2}

$$b=\dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} \qquad \text{and} \qquad a=\bar{y}-b\bar{x}$$

These estimates rely on several assumptions about the population of errors, $\varepsilon_i$, which are given on Page 5 of the Data Booklet.

\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Assumptions for the Errors,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=6.5cm]
\begin{center}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $\varepsilon_i$ are independent.
        \item $\text{E}(\varepsilon_i)=0$
        \item $\text{V}(\varepsilon_i)=\sigma^2$ where $\text{V}(Y)=\sigma^2$
    \end{itemize}
\end{center}
\end{tcolorbox}

\end{multicols}

\pagebreak

## Calculating the Value of a Residual

For any given $x_i$, the equation of the least squares regression line $\hat{Y_i}=a+bx_i$ gives a \textit{predicted} value $\hat{Y_i}$, also referred to as a \textit{fitted} value. For any observed data point $(x_i,y_i)$, the value of $y_i$ may be called the \textbf{data} value.

The difference between an observed data value $y_i$ and a corresponding fitted value $\hat{Y_i}$ is called a \textbf{residual}:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \draw[fill=black!10] (8.2,0.8) rectangle (13,2.3);
        \draw[very thick,dashed] (3,1) -- (3,2);
        \draw[semithick] (6,0) -- (0,2) node[left] {$\hat{Y_i}=a+bx_i$};
        \draw[fill=black] (3,2) circle (2pt) node[above] {$(x_i,y_i)$};
        \node[below] at (3,0.9) {$(x_i,\hat{Y}_i)$};
        \draw[dashed] (3,1) -- (3,2);
        \draw[fill] (3,1) circle (1pt);
        \draw[-stealth] (4.5,2) -- (3.2,2);
        \node[right] at (4.5,2) {Data, $y_i$};
        \draw[-stealth] (4.5,1) -- (3.2,1);
        \node[right] at (4.5,1) {Fitted, $\hat{Y_i}$};
        \draw[stealth-,dashed,thin] (3.2,1.5) -- (8,1.5);
        \node at (9.2,1.9) {Residual};
        \node at (10.25,1.85) {$=$};
        \node at (10.95,1.9) {Data};
        \node at (11.67,1.85) {$-$};
        \node at (12.25,1.9) {Fit};
        \node at (10.25,1.15) {$=$};
        \node at (10.95,1.2) {$y_i$};
        \node at (11.67,1.15) {$-$};
        \node at (12.25,1.2) {$\hat{Y_i}$};
        \node at (10.7,1.55) {\begin{minipage}{6cm}
            \begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Calculating a Residual,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black]
            \vspace{-0.5cm}
\begin{align*}
    \text{Residual}&=\text{Data}-\text{Fitted}\\
    &=\hspace{0.2cm}y_i\hspace{0.3cm}-\hspace{0.1cm}\hat{Y}_i
\end{align*}
\end{tcolorbox}
        \end{minipage}};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

For data points which lie \textit{above} the regression line, their residual is \textit{positive}.\newline This means that the observed value $y_i$ was \textit{greater} than that predicted by the regression line for the value of $x_i$.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]
        \draw[very thick] (0,0) -- (8,0.8) node[right] {$\hat{Y}=a+bx$};
        \draw[dashed] (1,1) -- (1,0.1);
        \draw[dashed] (2,-0.5) -- (2,0.2);
        \draw[thin,dashed] (3,0.7) -- (3,0.3);
        \draw[thin,dashed] (4,2) -- (4,0.4);
        \draw[thin,dashed] (5,-0.8) -- (5,0.5);
        \draw[thin,dashed] (6,1.2) -- (6,0.6);
        \draw[thin,dashed] (7,0) -- (7,0.7);
        \draw[fill] (1,1) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (3,0.7) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (4,2) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (6,1.2) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (2,-0.5) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (5,-0.8) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (7,0) circle (0.05);
        \node at (7,2) {\textit{positive residuals}};
        \node at (7,-1) {\textit{negative residuals}};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

For those which lie \textit{below} the line, their residual is \textit{negative}.\newline This means that the observed value $y_i$ was \textit{less} than that predicted by the regression line for the value of $x_i$.

To calculate a residual the fitted value, $\hat{Y}_i$ needs to first be found using the equation of the regression line.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!, colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:}

Students sitting a biology exam are given a mark for each of the two papers: theoretical ($x$) and practical ($y$). From a random sample of 12 students, the $y$ on $x$ regression line is calculated as $\hat{Y}=2.581+0.879x$. Calculate the residual for a student who gains:\\

\text{(a)   } 16 in the theoretical test and 18 in the practical test.\\[0.5em]
\text{(b)   } 10 in the theoretical test and 8 in the practical test.

\tcblower

\textbf{Solution:}

\vspace{-0.3cm}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \node[right] at (6.5,3.5) {(a)};
        \node[right] at (6.5,2.9) { $\hat{Y}=2.581+0.879\times 16=16.645$};
        \node[right] at (6.5,2.3) {Residual $=18-16.645=1.355$};
        \node[right] at (6.5,1.2) {(b)};
        \node[right] at (6.5,0.6) { $\hat{Y}=2.581+0.879\times 10=11.371$};
        \node[right] at (6.5,0) {Residual $=8-11.371=-3.371$};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (5.25,0) node[right] {$x$};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (0,3.25) node[above] {$y$}; 
        \node at (2.5,-1) {Theoretical mark};
        \node at (-2,1.75) {Practical};
        \node at (-2,1.25) {mark};
        \foreach \x in {0,1,...,5}{
        \draw (\x,0) -- (\x,-0.1);
        }
        \foreach \y in {0,0.6,...,3}{
        \draw (0,\y) -- (-0.1,\y);
        }
        \node[left] at (0,0) {0};
        \node[left] at (0,0.6) {5};
        \node[left] at (0,1.2) {10};
        \node[left] at (0,1.8) {15};
        \node[left] at (0,2.4) {20};
        \node[left] at (0,3.0) {25};
        \node[below] at (0,0) {0};
        \node[below] at (1,0) {5};
        \node[below] at (2,0) {10};
        \node[below] at (3,0) {15};
        \node[below] at (4,0) {20};
        \node[below] at (5,0) {25};
\draw[thick] (16*0.2,18*0.12) -- (16*0.2,16.8*0.12);
\draw[semithick] (3*0.2,5.488*0.12) -- (22*0.2,22.189*0.12);
\draw[stealth-] (10.6*0.2,9.5*0.12) -- (12*0.2,9*0.12) node[right] {$-3.371$};
\draw[stealth-] (15.3*0.2,18.1*0.12) -- (13.6*0.2,20*0.12) node[left] {$1.355$};
\draw[fill=black] (5*0.2,6*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill=black] (9*0.2,8*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill=black] (7*0.2,9*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill=black] (11*0.2,13*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill=black] (20*0.2,20*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill=black] (4*0.2,9*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill=black] (6*0.2,8*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill=black] (17*0.2,17*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill=black] (12*0.2,14*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill] (10*0.2,8*0.12) circle (0.05);
\draw[thick] (10*0.2,8*0.12) -- (10*0.2,11.6*0.12);
\draw[fill=black] (15*0.2,17*0.12) circle (0.02);
\draw[fill] (16*0.2,18*0.12) circle (0.05);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

## Estimating the Error Variance

The least squares regression model assumes that that the variance of the errors is a constant for all $x_i$ (and $Y_i$):

$$\text{V}(\varepsilon_i)=\sigma^2$$

The *error variance* is the variance of the population of data *relative to the regression line's predicted values*, indicated below by the arrows below. This is distinct from the *total variance* of $Y$, indicated by the single arrow to the right.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]
        \draw[thick] (0,2.13) -- (10,6.02) node[right] {$\hat{Y}=a+bx$};
        \draw[fill] (6.955968,6.2978298) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (7.912889,5.8402953) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (1.868432,1.6709538) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (6.571086,5.5025033) circle (0.05);

\draw[fill] (5.354720,5.4286745) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (5.692772,3.8360525) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (6.919847,4.0510383) circle (0.05);

\draw[fill] (1.814834,2.3950480) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (2.579615,3.8515882) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (8.730507,5.0549693) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (6.122615,4.5842622) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (5.604990,5.0227012) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (2.715744,4.0786740) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (4.666348,3.9671370) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (4.833494,2.6864497) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (2.699929,3.7831202) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (5.875923,4.1321580) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (1.141053,2.4180573) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (3.810106,4.7228958) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (3.983993,2.6769287) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (8.486224,3.7495115) circle (0.05);
    \draw[ultra thick,stealth-stealth] (1,1*0.39+2.13+1.1) -- (1,1*0.39+2.13-1.1);
    \draw[ultra thick,stealth-stealth] (3,3*0.39+2.13+1.1) -- (3,3*0.39+2.13-1.1);
    \draw[ultra thick,stealth-stealth] (5,5*0.39+2.13+1.1) -- (5,5*0.39+2.13-1.1);
    \draw[ultra thick,stealth-stealth] (7,7*0.39+2.13+1.1) -- (7,7*0.39+2.13-1.1);
    \draw[ultra thick,stealth-stealth] (9,9*0.39+2.13+1.1) -- (9,9*0.39+2.13-1.1);
    \draw[ultra thick,stealth-stealth] (15,4.1+2) -- (15,4.1-2);
    \node at (6,1) {constant variance of the errors $\epsilon_i$};
    \node at (15,1) {total variance of $Y$};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

The error variance $\sigma^2$ is estimated by:

\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Estimating the Error Variance,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=6cm]
\[s^2=\dfrac{\text{\text{SSR}}}{n-2}\]
\end{tcolorbox}

Where the (always positive) \textit{sum of squared residuals}, $\text{\text{SSR}}$, is calculated as:\hspace{0.5cm} $\text{\text{SSR}}=S_{yy}-\dfrac{(S_{xy})^2}{S_{xx}}$

Both the formula for estimating the error variance $\sigma^2$ and the more convenient second formula for the sum of squared residuals $\text{SSR}$ are given on page 5 of the Data Booklet.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} 

Students sitting a biology exam are given a mark for each of the two papers: theoretical ($x$) and practical ($y$). From a random sample of 12 students: $S_{xx}=290,S_{yy}=256.25,S_{xy}=255$.\\

\text{(a)   } Calculate the sum of squared residuals for this data set.\\[0.3em]
\text{(b)   } Obtain an estimate of the error variance.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}

\text{(a)   } $\text{SSR}=S_{yy}-\dfrac{(S_{xy})^2}{S_{xx}}=256.25-\dfrac{255^2}{290}=32.026$\\[0.7em]
\text{(b)   } $s^2=\dfrac{SRR}{n-2}=\dfrac{32.026}{12-2}=3.203$
\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

[]{#ex182q}

#### [Exercise 18.2](#ex182a) {-}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item A bivariate random sample has summary statistics:
\begin{center}
  \begin{tabular}{ccccccc}
      $S_{xx}=28.92$ & \quad & $S_{yy}=495.0$ & \quad & $S_{xy}=105.3$ & \quad & $n=14$
  \end{tabular}
\end{center}
It is determined that linear relationship appears to exist between the variables. 
\begin{enumerate}
        \item Obtain an estimate $s^2$ for the error variance, $\sigma^2$.
        \item Hence determine the value of $s$ for this bivariate data.
    \end{enumerate}

## The Coefficient of Determination

The *coefficient of determination*, $R^2$, is the proportion of the total variation in the response variable, $Y$, that is explained by the linear regression model. The higher the value of $R^2$, the less variation remains for $Y_i$ relative to the total variation of $Y$. This means that the coefficient of determination is a very useful measure of how 'good' a model is for the data.

\begin{center}
        \begin{tikzpicture}
            \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (5,0) node[right] {$x$};
            \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (0,3) node[above] {$y$};

            \draw[thick] (0.2,1.87+0.13*0.2) -- (4.8,1.87+0.13*4.8);
            \draw[fill=black] (0.2,1.53373841023011) circle (0.03);

\draw[fill=black] (3.4,1.76705018101233) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (3.6,1.86661649309301) circle (0.03);

            \node at (16,2.5) {$R^2=0.9604$};
            \draw[fill=black] (12.2,2.85798900535176) circle (0.03);
            \draw[thick] (12.2,3-0.54*0.2) -- (16.8,3-0.54*4.8);
\draw[fill=black] (12.4,2.60537287961341) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (12.6,2.77018510784324) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (12.8,2.66349862608913) circle (0.03);

\draw[fill=black] (14,1.81146604663732) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (14.2,2.08407903013156) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (14.4,1.50565270007012) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (14.6,1.46149356269359) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (14.8,1.39099215853279) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (15,1.64671625827524) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (15.2,1.21641922001981) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (15.4,1.46251390454929) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (15.6,0.938058516930767) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (15.8,0.825017334308726) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (16,0.823330020748936) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (16.2,0.687053814962354) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (16.4,0.731984300904749) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (16.6,0.450020978572811) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill=black] (16.8,0.301613948412619) circle (0.03);
\node[text width = 5.5cm,below right] at (0.1,-0.2) {12.5\% of the variation in $Y$ is explained by the linear model.};
\node[text width = 5.5cm,below right] at (12.1,-0.2) {96.0\% of the variation in $Y$ is explained by the linear model.};
\node at (8.5,1.7) {
\begin{minipage}{5.9cm}
\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Coefficient of Determination,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black]
$R^2=\dfrac{\text{explained variation}}{\text{total variation}}$
\end{tcolorbox}
\end{minipage}
};
        \end{tikzpicture}    
\end{center}

For the linear regression models covered in this course, the coefficient of determination is connected to the *correlation coefficient*, $r$, by a simple relationship:

\begin{center}
        \begin{tikzpicture}
        \node[left] at (0,0) {$r=\dfrac{S_{xy}}{\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{yy}}}$};
        \node at (2,0) {$\implies$};
        \node[right] at (4,0){
        \begin{minipage}{2.5cm}
        \begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black]
\vspace{-0.5cm}
\[R^2=r^2\]
\end{tcolorbox}
        \end{minipage}
};
        \end{tikzpicture}    
\end{center}

Both the formula above and the interpretation of the coefficient of determination implies that $0\leqslant R^2 \leqslant 1$.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!, colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} 

Students sitting a biology exam are given a mark for each of the two papers: theoretical ($x$) and practical ($y$). From a random sample of 12 students, the following summary statistics are obtained:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccccc}
        $S_{xx}=290$ & $S_{yy}=256.25$ & $S_{xy}=255$ & $n=12$
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

A linear regression model is proposed, and an equation for the regression line of $y$ on $x$ is obtained:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{c}
        $\hat{Y}=2.581+0.879x$
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

Calculate the coefficient of determination and interpret this value in context.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}

\vspace{-0.5cm}

\begin{align*}
r&=\dfrac{S_{xy}}{\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{yy}}}=\dfrac{255}{\sqrt{290\times 256.25}}=0.935\\[1em]
R^2&=r^2=0.935^2=0.8742
\end{align*}

This suggests that 87.4\% of the variation in practical scores can be explained by the linear model.

\end{tcolorbox}

The conclusion from the example above could also be phrased as:

"*87.4\% of the variation in practical scores can be explained by the theory scores.*"

\pagebreak

[]{#ex183q}

\end{enumerate}

\newpage

## Test for the Slope Parameter $\beta$

The value of the \textit{slope parameter}, $\beta$, cannot be known without obtaining a full population. The \textit{sample statistic} $b$ is an estimator for $\beta$, and may differ slightly or substantially from it, as shown below.

\end{multicols}

The slope parameter $\beta$ can be *positive*, *negative* or *zero*. Where $\beta=0$ it can be said that knowing $x_i$ has no value in predicting $y_i$. In other words, a linear regression model is only \textbf{useful for prediction} in cases where the slope parameter is \textit{non-zero}, or $\beta \ne 0$. The \textit{parametric} \textbf{Test for Beta} assesses whether data from a sample provide evidence to suggest that this is the case. The test statistic below follows a $t$-distribution with $n-2$ degrees of freedom, and in this course the test will always be \textit{two-tailed} with hypotheses $\text{H}_0: \beta=0$ and $\text{H}_1: \beta\ne 0$.

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.33\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{centering}
\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Test Statistic,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=5cm,height=2.5cm]
\vspace{-0.3cm}
\[t_{n-2}=\dfrac{b\sqrt{S_{xx}}}{s}\]
\end{tcolorbox}
\end{centering}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.66\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Conditions,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=12cm,height=2.5cm]
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\varepsilon_i$ are independent and identically distributed as $\text{N}(0,\sigma^2)$.
    \item The data was obtained through random sampling.
\end{itemize}
\end{tcolorbox}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}


\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!, colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} A bivariate sample of 8 values produces a regression line with equation $\hat{Y}=-0.8456x$. The following summary statistics were also obtained

\vspace{-0.3cm}
\begin{center}
  \begin{tabular}{ccccccccccc}
      $S_{xx}=157.875$ & \quad & $S_{yy}=188$ & \quad & $S_{xy}=-133.5$
  \end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.3cm}
Test at the $\alpha=0.1$ level for evidence that a linear regression model would be useful for prediction.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}\\

\begin{math}
\begin{array}{ll}
      H_0: & \beta=0\\
      H_1: & \beta \ne 0
    \end{array}
\left]
    \begin{array}{l}
      \alpha=0.1\\
      \textnormal{Two-tailed}
    \end{array}
  \right.
\end{math}

\vspace{0.3cm}

$\text{\text{SSR}}=S_{yy}-\dfrac{(S_{xy})^2}{S_{xx}}=188-\dfrac{(-133.5)^2}{157.875}=75.11, \quad s=\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{\text{SSR}}}{n-2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{75.11}{8-2}}=3.538$\\[2em]

Test statistic: $t=\dfrac{b\sqrt{S_{xx}}}{s}=\dfrac{-0.8456\times \sqrt{157.875}}{3.538}=-3.003$

\hspace{11.5cm}
\begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture,overlay]
\begin{axis}[
  no markers, 
  domain=-3:3, 
  samples=100,
  ymin=0,
  axis lines*=left, 
  every axis y label/.style={at=(current axis.above origin),anchor=south},
  every axis x label/.style={at=(current axis.right of origin),anchor=west},
  height=3cm, 
  width=6.5cm,
  xtick=\empty, 
  ytick=\empty,
  enlargelimits=false, 
  clip=false, 
  axis on top,
  grid = major,
  hide y axis
  ]
\addplot [draw=none, fill=black!70, domain=-3:-1.64] {gauss(x, 0, 1)} \closedcycle;
\addplot [draw=none, fill=black!70, domain=1.64:3] {gauss(x, 0, 1)} \closedcycle;
 \addplot [semithick] {gauss(x, 0, 1)};
\pgfmathsetmacro\valueA{gauss(-1.64,0,1)}
\pgfmathsetmacro\valueB{gauss(0,0,1)}
\draw [semithick, black]  (axis cs:-1.64,0) -- (axis cs:-1.64,\valueA);
\draw [semithick, black]  (axis cs:1.64,0) -- (axis cs:1.64,\valueA);
\node[below] at (axis cs: 0,0)  {$0$};
\node[above] at (axis cs: 0,\valueB)  {$t_6$};
\node[below] at (axis cs: -1.7, 0) {$-1.943$};
\node[above] at (axis cs: -2.2,\valueA) {5\%};
\node[below] at (axis cs: 1.7, 0) {$1.943$};
\node[above] at (axis cs: 2.2,\valueA) {5\%};
\end{axis}
\end{tikzpicture}\\

Critical value: $t_{6,0.95}=-1.943$\\

Since $-3.003<-1.943$, reject $\text{H}_0$ at the 10\% level of significance. There is  evidence to suggest that the slope parameter is not zero, so evidence that a linear model would be useful for prediction.

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

[]{#ex184q}

#### [Exercise 18.4](#ex184a) {-}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Six metal plates were immersed in a weak acid solution for various lengths of time. Their percentage losses in mass were then measured and a scatterplot drawn of time in solution ($x$ hours) against mass loss ($y$\%). The equation of the least squares regression line for $y$ on $x$ was obtained.\\[0.5em] 
    \textit{(See Context 1)}\\[0.5em]
    Perform a test at the 5\% level of significance to assess whether there is evidence to suggest that a linear model is useful for prediction.
    \item A scientist is investigating the effectiveness of a particular weed killer. She uses different concentrations of weed killer (mg/litre, $x$) and counts the number of surviving weeds (per 10 square metres, $y$) in a fixed area. The equation of the least squares regression line for $y$ on $x$ was obtained.\\[0.5em] 
    \textit{(See Context 5)}\\[0.5em]
    Assess at the 10\% level of significance whether the linear model is useful for prediction.

\pagebreak

## Test for the Correlation Coefficient $\rho$

The previously calculated correlation coefficient, $r$, is a sample statistic which measures the strength of the linear association between two variables. It is an estimate of the population parameter $\rho$ (\textit{``rho''}), where $-1\leqslant \rho \leqslant 1$. If $\rho=0$ then there is no \textit{linear} relationship between the variables. Given a sample of bivariate data for which there exists \textit{no} linear relationship, it is regardless common for the sample to give a \textit{non-zero} value for $r$:

\vspace{-0.3cm}
\begin{center}
        \begin{tikzpicture}
            \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (5,0) node[right] {$x$};
            \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (0,3) node[above] {$y$};

\vspace{-0.3cm}
The \textit{parametric} \textbf{Test for Rho} assesses a sample of bivariate data for evidence of a \textbf{linear association} between the variables, or $\rho\ne 0$. The test statistic below follows a $t_{n-2}$ distribution, and in this course the test will always be \textit{two-tailed} with hypotheses $\text{H}_0:\rho=0$ and $\text{H}_1:\rho\ne 0$.

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.33\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{centering}
\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Test Statistic,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=5cm,height=2.6cm]
\vspace{-0.3cm}
\[t_{n-2}=\dfrac{r\sqrt{n-2}}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}\]
\end{tcolorbox}
\end{centering}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.66\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Conditions,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=12cm,height=2.6cm]
\begin{itemize}
\setlength\itemsep{-0.3em}
    \item The variables are independent and follow an approximately \newline bivariate normal distribution.
    \item The data was obtained through random sampling.
\end{itemize}
\end{tcolorbox}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

Any conclusion should comment on the evidence for a linear association between the two variables.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!, colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} 

A bivariate sample has summary statistics:

\begin{center}
  \begin{tabular}{ccccccc}
      $S_{xx}=157.875$ & \qquad & $S_{yy}=188$ & \qquad & $S_{xy}=-133.5$ & \qquad & $n=8$
  \end{tabular}
\end{center}

Test at the 1\% significance level for evidence of a linear association between $x$ and $y$.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}\\[0.1em]

\begin{math}
\begin{array}{ll}
      H_0: & \rho=0\\
      H_1: & \rho \ne 0
    \end{array}
\left]
    \begin{array}{l}
      \alpha=0.01\\
      \textnormal{Two-tailed}
    \end{array}
  \right.
\end{math}

\vspace{0.3cm}

$r=\dfrac{S_{xy}}{\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{yy}}}=\dfrac{-133.5}{\sqrt{157.875\times 188}}=-0.7749$\\[1em]

Test statistic: $t=\dfrac{r\sqrt{n-2}}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}=\dfrac{-0.7749\sqrt{8-2}}{\sqrt{1-(-0.7749)^2}}=-3.003$
\hspace{1cm}
\begin{tikzpicture}[overlay]
\begin{axis}[
  no markers, 
  domain=-3:3, 
  samples=100,
  ymin=0,
  axis lines*=left, 
  every axis y label/.style={at=(current axis.above origin),anchor=south},
  every axis x label/.style={at=(current axis.right of origin),anchor=west},
  height=3cm, 
  width=6.5cm,
  xtick=\empty, 
  ytick=\empty,
  enlargelimits=false, 
  clip=false, 
  axis on top,
  grid = major,
  hide y axis
  ]
\addplot [draw=none, fill=black!70, domain=-3:-2.2] {gauss(x, 0, 1)} \closedcycle;
\addplot [draw=none, fill=black!70, domain=2.2:3] {gauss(x, 0, 1)} \closedcycle;
 \addplot [semithick] {gauss(x, 0, 1)};
\pgfmathsetmacro\valueA{gauss(-2.2,0,1)}
\pgfmathsetmacro\valueB{gauss(0,0,1)}
\draw [semithick, black]  (axis cs:-2.2,0) -- (axis cs:-2.2,\valueA);
\draw [semithick, black]  (axis cs:2.2,0) -- (axis cs:2.2,\valueA);
\node[below] at (axis cs: 0,0)  {$0$};
\node[above] at (axis cs: 0,\valueB)  {$t_6$};
\node[below] at (axis cs: -2.2, 0) {$-3.707$};
\node[above] at (axis cs: -2.4,\valueA) {0.5\%};
\node[below] at (axis cs: 2.2, 0) {$3.707$};
\node[above] at (axis cs: 2.4,\valueA) {0.5\%};
\end{axis}
\end{tikzpicture}\\

Critical value: $t_{6,0.995}=-3.707$\\

Since $-3.003>-3.707$, do not reject $\text{H}_0$ at the 1\% level of significance.\newline There is insufficient evidence to suggest that there is a linear relationship between the two variables.

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

\begin{enumerate}
    \item A researcher is wanting to know if there is a link between the number of calories ($x$) and the carbohydrate content ($y$) of different types of caffeinated drinks available at coffee shops. Summary statistics are obtained from a random sample of 22 caffeinated drink options from a range of coffee shops. \\[0.5em] 
    \textit{(See Context 2)}\\[0.5em]
    Perform a test at the 5\% level of significance to assess whether there is evidence to suggest that there is a linear relationship between the number of calories a caffeinated drink contains and its carbohydrate content.
    \item Researchers collect marks for 9 students randomly selected from a group of students taking both a maths test ($x$) and a physics test ($y$).\\[0.5em] 
    \textit{(See Context 4)}\\[0.5em]
    Perform a test at the 10\% level of significance to assess the linear association.
    \item A researcher laid a transect from the base of a tree and placed a quadrat at one metre intervals along the transect line. The number of seeds dropped by the tree contained in each quadrat was recorded.
     \begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \node[right] at (7,2.9) {Summary data for sample:};
        \node[right] at (7,2.2) {$\Sigma x=21$};
        \node[right] at (7,1.5) {$\Sigma x^2=91$};
        \node[right] at (7,0.8) {$S_{yy}=54.833$};
        \node[right] at (7,0.1) {$S_{xy}=3.5$};
        \node[right] at (7,-0.6) {$n=6$};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (5.25,0) node[right] {$x$};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (0,3.25) node[above] {$y$}; 
        \node at (2.5,-1) {Distance from base (metres)};
        \node at (-2,1.5) {No. of seeds};
        \foreach \x in {0,0.8,...,4.8}{
        \draw (\x,0) -- (\x,-0.1);
        }
        \foreach \y in {0,0.6,...,3}{
        \draw (0,\y) -- (-0.1,\y);
        }
        \node[left] at (0,0) {0};
        \node[left] at (0,0.6) {2};
        \node[left] at (0,1.2) {4};
        \node[left] at (0,1.8) {6};
        \node[left] at (0,2.4) {8};
        \node[left] at (0,3) {10};
        \node[below] at (0,0) {0};
        \node[below] at (0.8,0) {1};
        \node[below] at (1.6,0) {2};
        \node[below] at (2.4,0) {3};
        \node[below] at (3.2,0) {4};
        \node[below] at (4.0,0) {5};
        \node[below] at (4.8,0) {6};
        \draw[fill=black] (0.8,0*0.3) circle (0.06);
        \draw[fill=black] (1.6,9*0.3) circle (0.06);
        \draw[fill=black] (2.4,4*0.3) circle (0.06);
        \draw[fill=black] (3.2,3*0.3) circle (0.06);
        \draw[fill=black] (4,7*0.3) circle (0.06);
        \draw[fill=black] (4.8,3*0.3) circle (0.06);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
        Calculate the sample product moment correlation coefficient and test at the 10\% significance level for a linear association between the variables.
    \item When a type of metal bar is heated it expands. The amount by which it expands (in mm) and the increase in temperature (in $^{\circ}\text{C}$) for a random sample of 6 such bars heated to specific temperatures is recorded.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \node[right] at (7,2.9) {Summary data for sample:};
        \node[right] at (7,2.2) {$r=0.981$};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (5.25,0) node[right] {$x$};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (0,3.25) node[above] {$y$}; 
        \node at (2.5,-1) {Temperature rise ($^{\circ}\text{C}$)};

        Use a parametric hypothesis test to assess, at the 0.1\% level of significance, the evidence for a linear association between temperature rise and expansion.
\end{enumerate}

[]{#ex185q}

#### [Exercise 18.5](#ex185a) {-}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item For each of Contexts 1 to 5, calculate the coefficient of determination and interpret it in context.
    \item The age in months of 30 randomly selected primary school pupils ($x$) and the length of their right foot in centimetres ($y$) were recorded, with the following summary data obtained:
     \begin{center}
        \begin{tabular}{cccccc}
            $\Sigma x=2964$ & $\Sigma x^2=294248$ & $\Sigma y=577.3$ & $\Sigma y^2=11152.71$ & $\Sigma xy=57262.7$ & $n=30$
        \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Calculate the coefficient of determination.
        \item Interpret the result from (a).
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\newpage

## Regression Line of $x$ on $y$

Each least squares regression line met so far has been an $y$ *on* $x$ regression line, calculated with the aim of minimising errors when *predicting* $y$ *values*. However, a $y$ *on* $x$ regression line model cannot be used to predict $x$ values.

If the prediction of $x$ values based on $y$ values is of interest, it is necessary to obtain the equation of an $x$ *on* $y$ regression line. This takes a different form and different values of $a$ and $b$ as shown below:

\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Calculating an $x$ on $y$ Regression Line,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=11cm]
$\hat{X}=a+by \qquad \text{where} \qquad b=\dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{yy}} \qquad \text{and} \qquad a=\bar{x}-b\bar{y}$
\end{tcolorbox}

\vspace{-0.1cm}

\begin{multicols}{2}
    \begin{center}
        \begin{tikzpicture}

            \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (5,0) node[right] {$x$};
            \node at (2.5,-0.5) {Prediction with a $y$ on $x$ model};
            \draw[fill] (1.8717487,1.7974477) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (2.7352247,2.0554359) circle (0.05);

            \draw[fill] (1.8717487,1.7974477) circle (0.05);

\draw[fill] (2.3668546,2.4227856) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (2.7736595,2.6725544) circle (0.05);
\draw[fill] (2.0828944,1.8844629) circle (0.05);
        \draw[very thin,dashed,black!30] (0.5,0.5*0.82+0.43) -- (4,4*0.82+0.43) node[right] {$y$ on $x$};
        \draw[very thick] (0.5*0.9+0.16,0.5) -- (4*0.9+0.16,4) node[above] {$x$ on $y$};
        \draw[-Latex,dashed] (0,3.25) -- (3.25*0.9+0.16,3.25) -- (3.25*0.9+0.16,0);
        \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
\end{multicols}

\vspace{-0.3cm}

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!, colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} 

Students sitting a biology exam are given a mark for each of the two papers: theoretical ($x$) and practical ($y$). From a random sample of 12 students, the following summary statistics are obtained:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccccc}
        $\Sigma x=132$ & $\Sigma x^2 =1742$ & $\Sigma y=147$ & $\Sigma y^2=2057$ & $\Sigma xy=1872$\\[1em]
        $S_{xx}=290$ & $S_{yy}=256.25$ & $S_{xy}=255$ & $n=12$ & $r=0.935$
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

Determine the equation for the least squares regression line of $x$ on $y$, and hence predict the theory paper mark for a student who has only sat the practical paper and obtained a mark of 17 in it.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}\\

$b=\dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{yy}}=\dfrac{255}{256.25}=0.995$\\[0.1em]

$a=\bar{x}-b\bar{y}=\dfrac{132}{12}-0.995\times \dfrac{147}{12}=-1.189$\\[0.1em]

$\hat{X}=-1.189+0.995y$\\[0.5em]

For a student with a practical mark ($y$) of 17:\\[0.1em]

$\hat{X}=-1.189+0.995\times 17=15.7$

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

[]{#ex186q}

#### [Exercise 18.6](#ex186a) {-}

For each of Contexts 1 to 5, determine the equation of the least squares regression line for $x$ on $y$, and hence calculate predicted values for each of the following.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item From Context 1: The time a metal plate spent in the acid solution if the mass loss was 2.1\%.
    \item From Context 2: The calories contained in a caffeinated drink which has a carbohydrate content of 34 grams.
    \item From Context 3: The systolic blood pressure of a patient whose body fat \% is 11.
    \item From Context 4: The maths mark of a student who gained a physics mark of 75.
    \item From Context 5: The weed killer concentration used if the area contained 8 weeds per $10\text{m}^2$.
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

## Residual Plots

When considering the use of a linear regression model for bivariate data, it is recommended to produce a plot of *residuals* ($\varepsilon_i$) against *fitted values* ($\hat{Y_i}$), which have been calculated using the sample regression line. The purpose of this is both to ascertain whether a linear regression model is *the most appropriate* model, as well as to judge the validity of several commonly required assumptions, including those required to obtain the estimates for $\alpha$ and $\beta$:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccc}
        $\text{E}(\varepsilon_i)=0$ & $\quad$ \text{and} $\quad$ & $\text{V}(\varepsilon_i)=\sigma^2$
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

Below is an illustration of a standard scatterplot showing a sample of bivariate data on the left, with a $y$ on $x$ regression line included, and a residual plot for the same data on the right. The data points highlighted with larger circles on the $y$ against $x$ scatterplot have their residuals similarly highlighted on the residual plot.

\begin{multicols}{2}

\vspace{-0.5cm}
It should be observed that the residual plot broadly appears to show:

\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=Interpreting Residual Plots,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=11cm]
\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccc}
        A random scatter of points... & $\qquad$ & \\
        ...centred on zero... & & $\text{E}(\varepsilon_i)=0$\\
        ...with a constant variance. & & $\text{V}(\varepsilon_i)=\sigma^2$
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}
\end{tcolorbox}

This means that the residual plot would give no reason to doubt the assumptions made regarding the errors. This *ideal* residual plot would suggest that the linear regression model is **appropriate**.

A helpful way of visualising that the residuals are *centred on zero* with a *constant variance* is to view the residual plot as a series of isolated *vertical strips*, as shown below:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[xscale=2]
        \draw[fill=black!10] (3,-2.5) rectangle (9,2.55);
        \draw[fill=white] (3.2,-2.3) rectangle (8.8,2.35);
        \foreach \x in {3.2,4,...,8.8}{
        \draw (\x,-2.3) -- (\x,2.35);
        }
        \draw[dashed] (3,0) -- (9,0);
        \foreach \x in {3.6,4.4,...,8.4}{
        \draw[ultra thick,Latex-Latex,black!10] (\x,1.2) -- (\x,-1.2);
        } 
        \draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (8.39307409253776,1.06597870442773) ellipse (0.015 and 0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (8.32968519061489,-1.61965740143367) ellipse (0.015 and 0.03);

The residual plot appears to show a `funnel' shaped pattern of points centred on zero, with variance increasing as the fitted value increases. 

\vspace{-1cm}
\begin{center}
    \begin{align*}
        \text{E}(\varepsilon_i)&=0\\
        \text{V}(\varepsilon_i)&\ne\sigma^2
    \end{align*}
\end{center}

This suggests the that linear model is \textbf{not appropriate}.
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

\vspace{-0.3cm}

  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.56\textwidth}
The residual plot appears to show a pattern of points following a curve, with constant variance.

\vspace{-1cm}
\begin{center}
    \begin{align*}
        \text{E}(\varepsilon_i)&\ne0\\
        \text{V}(\varepsilon_i)&=\sigma^2
    \end{align*}
\end{center}

This suggests that the linear model is \textbf{not appropriate}.
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (8.59999999999999,-0.667504493806603) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (8.64999999999999,-0.700788960824055) circle (0.03);

\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (8.74999999999999,-0.752671839665586) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (8.79999999999999,-0.790639677383648) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (8.84999999999999,-0.84454240842857) circle (0.03);

\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (8.95,) circle (0.03);

    \node at (6,-2) {Fitted value};
    \node[rotate=90] at (2.2,0) {Residual};
    \node[left] at (3,0) {$0$};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.56\textwidth}
The residual plot appears to show a pattern of points following a curve, with variance decreasing as the fitted value increases.

\vspace{-1cm}
\begin{center}
    \begin{align*}
        \text{E}(\varepsilon_i)&\ne0\\
        \text{V}(\varepsilon_i)&\ne \sigma^2
    \end{align*}
\end{center}

This suggests that the linear model is \textbf{not appropriate}.
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

\vspace{-0.3cm}

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]

\vspace{-0.5cm}
Use the residual plot to comment on the validity of the linear model.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:} The residual plot appears to show a random scatter of points centred on zero, with a broadly constant variance as the fitted value increases. This suggests the linear model between stopping distance and speed could be appropriate.

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} The mark scored in a Maths test ($x$) and an English test ($y$) is recorded for a random sample of pupils. A linear regression model between Maths mark and English mark is proposed, the equation of the $y$ on $x$ regression line obtained and a residual plot is constructed, shown below along with the tabulated data.

\begin{multicols}{2}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
        \hline
        \multirow{2}{*}{Pupil} & Maths & English & Fit & Residual\\
          & $x_i$ & $y_i$ & $\hat{Y_i}$ & $y_i-\hat{Y_i}$\\
        \hline
        A & 1 & 3 & 6.98 & -3.98\\
        B & 8 & 14 & 10.24 & \textcolor{white}{-}3.76\\
        C & 15 & 8 & 13.50 & -5.50\\
        D & 18 & 20 & 14.90 & \textcolor{white}{-}5.09\\
        E & 23 & 19 & 17.23 & \textcolor{white}{-}1.77\\
        F&28&	17&	19.56&	-2.56\\
        G&	33&	27	&21.89&	\textcolor{white}{-}5.11\\
        H	&39	&26&	24.48&	\textcolor{white}{-}1.32\\
        I&	45	&21&	27.48&	-6.48\\
        J	&45	&29	&27.48	&\textcolor{white}{-}1.52\\
        \hline
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \draw (0,-2.5) rectangle (6,2.5);
        \draw[dashed] (0,0) -- (6,0);
        \node at (3,2.75) {Residual plot};
        \node[rotate=90] at (-0.75,0) {Residuals};
        \node[left] at (0,0) {$0$};
        \node at (3,-2.75) {Fitted values};
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (0.24570478532022,-0.796563828256176) circle (0.05);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (1.06088828256176,0.751289373950588) circle (0.05);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (1.87607177980331,-1.10085742384265) circle (0.05);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (2.22543613576397,1.01965109138882) circle (0.05);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (2.80771006236508,0.35383195010794) circle (0.05);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (3.38998398896618,-0.511987191172943) circle (0.05);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (3.97225791556728,1.02219366754617) circle (0.05);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (4.67098662748861,0.263210698009115) circle (0.05);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (5.36971533940993,-1.29577227152794) circle (0.05);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (5.36971533940993,0.304227728472056) circle (0.05);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{multicols}

\vspace{-0.5cm}
Use the residual plot to comment on the validity of the linear model.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:} The residual plot appears to show a random scatter of points centred on zero, and variance appears to be plausibly constant. The residual plot does not contradict any of the required assumptions for the errors, and so a linear model between English mark and Maths mark can be considered appropriate.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} A 1920s study on automobile safety involved recording the stopping distances (in feet) from selected speeds (in miles per hour) for a random sample of 47 cars. Below is a scatterplot of the results with a regression line obtained from a linear model, and a residual plot.

\begin{multicols}{2}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
    \draw (0,0) -- (5,0);
    \draw (0,0) -- (0,5);
        \draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (0.8,0.08) circle (0.03);
        \foreach \x in {0,5,...,25}{
        \draw (0.2*\x,0) -- (0.2*\x,-0.1);
        \node[below] at (0.2*\x,0) {\x};
        }
        \foreach \y in {0,24,...,120}{
        \node[left] at (0,0.04*\y) {\y};
        \draw (0,0.04*\y) -- (-0.1,0.04*\y);
        }
        \node at (2.5,-0.75) {Speed (mph)};
        \node[rotate=90] at (-1,2.5) {Stopping distance (ft)};
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (0.8,0.4) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (1.4,0.16) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (1.4,0.88) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (1.6,0.64) circle (0.03);

\vspace{-0.5cm}
Use the residual plot to comment on the validity of the linear model.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:} The residual plot appears to show a random scatter of points centred on zero, with a broadly constant variance as the fitted value increases. This suggests a linear model between stopping distance and speed could be appropriate.

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

\begin{enumerate}[resume]
    \item Metal cables are tested under laboratory conditions to determine their breaking strength. Each cable is fixed between two points, and masses attached to the middle of the cable, with the mass steadily increased until the cable breaks. The scatterplot below shows the cable diameter ($x$ mm) against the maximum mass held before breaking ($y$ kg).
    \begin{multicols}{2}

        \draw (0,\y) -- (-0.1,\y);
        }

        \draw (\x,-0.1) -- (\x,0) node[below] {\x};

    \item 31 randomly selected black cherry trees have their diameter at a set distance from the ground measured, in inches, with the height of each tree also measured, in feet.     \item A student investigating the relationship between the length of a pendulum and the time taken for one complete swing to occur made a simple pendulum from string and a metal disc, suspended from a clamp over the edge of a table. The time taken for one complete swing ($y$ seconds) was recorded for 12 different string lengths ($x$ cm).
    \begin{multicols}{2}
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \draw (0,0) -- (5,0);
        \foreach \x in {0,10,...,50}{
        \draw (0.1*\x,-0.1) -- (0.1*\x,0) node[below] {\x};
        }
        \node at (2.5,-1) {Length (cm)};
        \draw (0,0) -- (0,3.2);
        \foreach \y in {0,0.5,...,1.5}{
        \draw (-0.1,2*\y) -- (0,2*\y) node[left] {\y};
        }
        \node[rotate=90] at (-1,1.6) {Time (seconds)};
        \draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (0.65,1.02) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (1.1,1.36) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (1.32,1.46) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (1.5,1.58) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!80,fill=black!80] (1.8,1.76) circle (0.03);

\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

## Transforming Data

Where a \textit{non-linear} relationship seems to exist between two variables then it would not be appropriate to use a model which describes a \textit{linear} relationship between them. The presence of a non-linear relationship may be immediately clear from an initial scatterplot, or it may only be revealed once a residual plot for a proposed linear model is created.

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.4\textwidth}
\vspace{-0.5cm}

A linear model might initially seem to describe well relationship between $x$ and $y$, as might the result of $R^2=0.9228$.\\

However, the residual plot suggests that a\newline linear model between $x$ and $y$ is \textit{not} appropriate, since the residuals follow a curved pattern.

\vspace{-0.6cm}

The simulated data above was actually generated using a \textit{quadratic} relationship between $x$ and $y$.\newline A scatterplot of $\sqrt{y}$ against $x$ for this data is shown below, alongside a residual plot:

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.4\textwidth}
\vspace{-0.5cm}

Both the scatterplot and the residual plot for this new model of $\sqrt{y}$ on $x$ suggest that it is appropriate for the data.\\

This new, better-fitting model also produces an improved value for the coefficient of determination, of $R^2=0.9402$.

\end{minipage}%

\vspace{-0.5cm}

Creating a model such as this, in which one or both variables have some operation performed on them (such as taking the \textit{square root}) is called applying a \textbf{transformation} to the data. In practice, knowing which transformation(s) to try will generally be guided by a background understanding of the variables, or previous studies. In this course, any required transformations will be clearly specified.

The value of applying transformations in this way, where possible, is that linear regression can hence be extended to nonlinear relationships.

Continuing with the example of the model of the *square root* of $y$ *on* $x$, the various calculations required for linear regression can be *translated* in terms of $x$ and $\sqrt{y}$, instead of $x$ and $y$, such as:

Equation of the $\sqrt{y}$ *on* $x$ regression line: $\hat{\sqrt{Y}}=ax+b$ where $b=\dfrac{S_{x\sqrt{y}}}{S_{xx}}$ and $a=\overline{\sqrt{y}}+bx$.

Sample correlation coefficient: $r=\dfrac{S_{x\sqrt{y}}}{\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{\sqrt{y}\sqrt{y}}}}$.

The sum of squared residuals is $\text{SSR}=S_{\sqrt{y}\sqrt{y}}-\dfrac{S^2_{x\sqrt{y}}}{S_{xx}}$ and an estimate for $\sigma^2$ is given by $\dfrac{\text{SSR}}{n-2}$.

Predictions may be made using an $\sqrt{y}$ *on* $x$ regression line.\newline Any value of $\sqrt{y}$ obtained must be squared to find a predicted value for $y$ itself for any given $x$ value.

\pagebreak

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!, colframe=RoyalBlue]

\textbf{Problem:} Data was obtained for a sample of 10 cars of the stopping distance ($y$ metres) when travelling at various speeds ($x$ kilometres per hour). \textbf{Table 1} below shows the results.

\begin{center}
    \textbf{Table 1}
    \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
        \hline
        Speed ($x$) & 8.0 & 12.9 & 12.9 & 24.1 & 29.0 & 33.8 & 41.8 & 48.3 & 56.3 & 64.4\\
        \hline
        Distance ($y$) & 2.4 & 4.0 & 3.4 & 4.9 & 10.4 & 11.9 & 12.5 & 20.4 & 32.6 & 33.5\\
        \hline
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

The line $\hat{Y}=-5.11+0.5697x$ was fitted, and a scatterplot and a residual plot drawn (\textbf{Figures 1 and 2}).

\vspace{-0.2cm}

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.5\textwidth}
  \centering
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \draw[RoyalBlue!1!] (-1,-1) rectangle (7,3.3);
        \node at (3,3) {\textbf{Figure 1}};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (6.5,0) node[below] {$x$};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (0,3) node[left] {$y$};
        \node at (3,-0.8) {speed};
        \node[rotate=90] at (-0.8,1.25) {distance};
        \foreach \x in {10,20,...,60}{
        \draw (0.1*\x,0) -- (0.1*\x,-0.1) node[below] {$\scriptstyle{\x}$};
        };
        \foreach \y in {10,20,30}{
        \draw (0,0.08*\y) -- (-0.1,0.08*\y) node[left] {$\scriptstyle{\y}$};
        };
        \draw[fill] (0.1*8.0,0.08*2.4) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (0.1*12.9,0.08*4.0) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (0.1*12.9,0.08*3.4) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (0.1*24.1,0.08*4.9) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (0.1*29.0,0.08*10.4) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (0.1*33.8,0.08*11.9) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (0.1*41.8,0.08*12.5) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (0.1*48.3,0.08*20.4) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (0.1*56.3,0.08*32.6) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (0.1*64.4,0.08*33.5) circle (0.05);
        \draw[thick] (0.1*9,-0.08*5.11+0.08*0.5697*9) -- (0.1*65,-0.08*5.11+0.08*0.5697*65);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.5\textwidth}

        \draw[RoyalBlue!1!] (-1.5,-2.5) rectangle (6.5,1.8);

\vspace{-0.5cm}

A second linear model was created between speed ($x$) and the square root of the stopping distance ($\sqrt{y}$). For this new model, a scatterplot (\textbf{Figure 3}) and residual plot (\textbf{Figure 4}) were produced, and a least squares regression line was computed as: \texttt{square root of distance = 0.797 + 0.078 $\times$ speed}

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.5\textwidth}
  \centering

        \draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (0,3) node[left] {$\sqrt{y}$};

        \foreach \x in {10,20,...,60}{

        };
        \foreach \y in {1,2,...,5}{

\begin{minipage}[c]{.5\textwidth}

        \draw (0.2*\x,-1.7) -- (0.2*\x,-1.75);

        \draw[fill] (1.8032,2*0.1968) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (1.8032,2*0.04070889145858) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (2.6768,2*-0.46320563788213) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (3.1,2*0.16590309931942) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (3.4334,2*0.01623766213207) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (4.0574,2*-0.52186609406726) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (4.5644,2*-0.04776408374551) circle (0.05);
        \draw[fill] (5.1884,2*0.52124096944808) circle (0.05);

\end{figure}

\vspace{-0.5cm}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(a)] Comment on the appropriateness of the linear model of $y$ on $x$.
    \item[(b)] Comment on the suitability of the new model of $\sqrt{y}$ on $x$.
    \item[(c)] By first creating a new table for $x$ and $\sqrt{y}$ and finding summary statistics for this data, calculate the coefficient of determination for this new model and interpret its value.
    \item[(d)] Estimate the stopping distance at a speed of 50 kilometres per hour.
\end{enumerate}

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(a)] The residual plot appears to show a curved pattern, so this linear model is not suitable.
    \item[(b)] The residual plot for the new model appears to show a random scatter of points centered on zero, with a constant variance as fitted values increase, so the new model seems appropriate.
    \item[(c)] $r=\dfrac{S_{x\sqrt{y}}}{\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{\sqrt{y}\sqrt{y}}}}=\dfrac{264.36}{\sqrt{3369.025\times 21.586}}=0.9803$ and $R^2=0.9803^2=0.961$.\newline
    $96.1$\% of the variation of $\sqrt{\text{distance}}$ is explained by the model.
    \item[(d)] $\sqrt{\text{distance}}=0.797+0.078\times 50=4.697\text{, so distance}=4.697^2=22.1\text{ metres}$
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}

\vspace{-0.5cm}
\begin{enumerate}[resume]
    \item Comment on the suitability of this new model of $y$ on $x$.
\end{enumerate}
The new model has summary statistics of $\Sigma x=225,\;\Sigma y =6.564,\;\Sigma x^2=7125,\;S_{xy}=14.474,\;n=0$.
\begin{enumerate}[resume]
    \item Find the new least squares regression line of $y$ on $x$.
    \item Estimate the tool-wear corresponding to a run-time of 32 minutes.\newline (Hint: $\log_a{t}=b$ can be written as $a^b=t$).
\end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

## Confidence Intervals and Prediction Intervals
One purpose for adopting a regression approach to examine the relationship between two variables is the wish to make predictions related to the response variable ($Y$) when given a particular value for the predictor ($x$). 

The least squares regression line equation of $\hat{Y_i}=a+bx_i$ gives an *estimate* for the **mean** value of the response variable for any given $x_i$, whilst the *true* mean response value for a given $x_i$, which is denoted $\text{E}(Y_i\,|\,x_i)$, is unknown. 

It can be desirable to obtain a **confidence interval** for $\text{E}(Y_i\,|\,x_i)$.

\begin{minipage}[c]{.55\textwidth}
It can be shown that the \textit{point estimate} for the mean response, $\hat{Y_i}$, has a sample standard error of:

$$s\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}+\dfrac{(x_i-\bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}}$$

If the errors, $\varepsilon_i$, are identically and independently distributed as $\text{N}\left(0,\sigma^2\right)$ then the formula below, given on Page 5 of the Data Booklet, can be derived.
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}


\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=A Confidence Interval for the Mean Response,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=15.5cm]
A $100(1-\alpha)\%$ \text{CI} for $\text{E}(Y_i\,|\,x_i)$ is given by: $\hat{Y_i}\,\pm\,t_{n-2,1-\alpha/2}\times s\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}+\dfrac{(x_i-\bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}}$
\end{tcolorbox}

With all confidence intervals, the level of confidence relates to the success rate of the *procedure used to generate the interval*, rather than the probability of a calculated interval containing the mean response value $\text{E}(Y_i\,|\,x_i)$.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!, colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} 

The lengths in centimetres ($x$) and masses in kilograms ($y$) of 12 salmon were measured, with the results:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccccc}
        $\Sigma x=30$ & $\Sigma y=540$ & $S_{xx}=12$ & $S_{yy}=4800$ & $S_{xy}=150$
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

Construct a 95\% confidence interval for the mean length of salmon with mass 3kg.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}\\

$b=\dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}=\dfrac{150}{12}=12.5, \quad a=\bar{y}-b\bar{x}=\dfrac{540}{12}-12.5\times \dfrac{30}{12}=13.75 \quad \implies \quad \hat{Y_i}=13.75+12.5x_i$\\[0.3em]

$\hat{Y}=13.75+12.5\times 3=51.75$\\[0.3em]

$\text{\text{SSR}}=S_{yy}-\dfrac{(S_{xy})^2}{S_{xx}}=4800-\dfrac{150^2}{12}=2925, \quad s^2=\dfrac{\text{\text{SSR}}}{n-2}=\dfrac{2925}{10}=292.5, \quad s=\sqrt{292.5}=17.1$\\[0.3em]

A 95\% CI for $\text{E}(Y\,|\,x=3)$ is $51.75\,\pm\,2.228 \times 17.1\times \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{12}+\dfrac{(3-2.5)^2}{12}}=51.75\,\pm\,12.30$\\[0.3em]

So a 95\% confidence interval for the mean length of a 3kg salmon is $(39.45\text{cm}\,,\,64.05\text{cm})$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

Instead of an interval for the value of \textit{mean} response, it may be desired to obtain an interval for **individual** response values for a given $x_i$, denoted as $Y_i\,|\,x_i$. 

The desired interval is called a **prediction interval**.

\draw[fill] (2.2863092,2.8204645) circle (0.025);
\draw[fill] (3.5728943,1.9649286) circle (0.025);
\draw[fill] (3.4744290,2.3868745) circle (0.025);
\draw[fill] (3.1110311,2.3838065) circle (0.025);
\draw[fill] (3.1142563,2.4495563) circle (0.025);
\draw[fill] (1.4003549,1.6048878) circle (0.025);
        \end{tikzpicture}
        \end{center}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.4\textwidth}

Since the value of a randomly sampled individual can be expected to have more variability than the value of a sample mean, a wider interval around $\hat{Y_i}$ is needed.\\[0.5em]

Note the more darkly shaded region lying closer to the regression line, showing the narrower confidence interval for the same data.\\[0.5em]

In the long-run, a 95\% prediction interval for $Y_i\,|\,x_i$, such as indicated in the diagram, will tend to capture 95\% of all observed response values for any given $x_i$.

\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=A Prediction Interval for an Individual Response,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=15cm]
A $100(1-\alpha)\%$ \text{PI} for $Y_i\,|\,x_i$ is given by: $\hat{Y_i}\,\pm\,t_{n-2,1-\alpha/2}\times s\sqrt{1+\dfrac{1}{n}+\dfrac{(x_i-\bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}}$
\end{tcolorbox}

This formula, also given on Page 5 of the Data Booklet, relies also on the errors, $\varepsilon_i$, being identically and independently distributed as $\text{N}\left(0,\sigma^2\right)$

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!, colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} 

The lengths in centimetres ($x$) and masses in kilograms ($y$) of 12 salmon were measured, with the results:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccccc}
        $\Sigma x=30$ & $\Sigma y=540$ & $S_{xx}=12$ & $S_{yy}=4800$ & $S_{xy}=150$
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

Construct a 95\% prediction interval for the length of salmon with mass 3kg.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}\\

$b=\dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}=\dfrac{150}{12}=12.5, \quad a=\bar{y}-b\bar{x}=\dfrac{540}{12}-12.5\times \dfrac{30}{12}=13.75 \quad \implies \quad \hat{Y_i}=13.75+12.5x_i$\\[0.5em]

$\hat{Y}=13.75+12.5\times 3=51.75$\\[0.5em]

$\text{\text{SSR}}=S_{yy}-\dfrac{(S_{xy})^2}{S_{xx}}=4800-\dfrac{150^2}{12}=2925, \quad s^2=\dfrac{\text{\text{SSR}}}{n-2}=\dfrac{2925}{10}=292.5, \quad s=\sqrt{292.5}=17.1$\\[0.5em]

A 95\% PI for $Y\,|\,x=3$ is $51.75\,\pm\,2.228 \times 17.1\times \sqrt{1+\dfrac{1}{12}+\dfrac{(3-2.5)^2}{12}}=51.75\,\pm\,40.03$\\[0.5em]

So a 95\% prediction interval for the observed length of a 3kg salmon is $(11.72\text{cm}\,,\,91.78\text{cm})$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

[]{#ex188q}

#### [Exercise 18.9](#ex189a) {-}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item From Context 1: Construct a 95\% confidence interval for the mean \% mass loss of a metal plate in the acid solution for 150 hours.
    \item From Context 2: Construct a 90\% confidence interval for the mean carbohydrate content for a caffeinated drink with 400 calories.
    \item From Context 3: Construct a 99\% prediction interval for the body fat \% for a patient with systolic blood pressure of 130.
    \item From Context 4: Construct a 95\% prediction interval for the physics mark of a student who gained a maths mark of 74.
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

[]{#re18q}

\begin{enumerate}

    \item When a type of metal bar is heated it expands. The amount by which it expands ($e$, in mm) and for a given increase in temperature ($t$, in $\degree \text{C}$) is recorded for a random sample of six such bars. A regression line with equation is $\hat{E}=0.23+0.077t$ is obtained for the data, as well as summary statistics.

\item A running website randomly selected eleven amateur runners who recently completed a marathon and asked them to complete a survey which included their finishing time in the marathon and information about their training routine. The scatterplot below shows, for each runner, their finishing time ($x$, in hours) and the total distance they ran in the final four weeks of their training ($y$, in miles). 

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
\foreach \x in {0,1,...,7}{
\draw (\x,0) -- (\x,-0.1);
\node[below] at (\x,0) {\x};
}
\foreach \y in {0,25,...,175}{
\draw (0,0.02*\y) -- (-0.1,0.02*\y);
\node[left] at (0,0.02*\y) {\y};
}
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (2.4,2.7) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (2.9,2.84) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (3.1,2.42) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (3.7,2.3) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (3.9,1.9) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (4,2.08) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (4.2,1.88) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (4.4,1.74) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (4.7,1.62) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (5,1.18) circle (0.03);
\draw[black!70,fill=black!70] (6.2,1.3) circle (0.03);
\draw[thick] (2,2.96) -- (6.5,0.84);
\draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (7,0) node[right] {$x$};
\draw[-stealth] (0,0) -- (0,3.6) node[above] {$y$};
\node at (3.5,-1) {Time (hours)};
\node at (-2,2.5) {Distance};
\node at (-2,2) {(miles)};
\node[right] at (9,4) {Summary statistics:};
\node[right] at (9,3.4) {$\sum x=44.5$};
\node[right] at (9,2.8) {$\sum x^2=191.21$};
\node[right] at (9,2.2) {$\sum y=1098$};
\node[right] at (9,1.6) {$\sum y^2=116768$};
\node[right] at (9,1) {$\sum xy=4179.2$};
\node[right] at (9,0.4) {$n=11$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}  

A least squares regression line for $y$ on $x$ is shown on the graph, with equation $\hat{Y}=194.8-23.48x$.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(a)] Calculate the correlation coefficient, and comment on its value.
\end{enumerate}

The website wishes to allow users the ability to input the number of miles in their training plan for the four weeks leading up to the rate, and receive in response an estimate for their finishing time.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(b)] Explain why the equation provided above is unsuitable for this purpose.
    \item[(c)] Obtain a suitable equation which could instead be used.
\end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak
